lesson.menu_desc: Share opinions and preferences in a professional chat -- I think / I believe / I prefer / I would rather -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 14 -- Opinions & Preferences -- giving opinions and stating preferences
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rubens Tofolo | Aula 14 -- Opinions & Preferences

listenings.text: Welcome to this short reflection on the morning sessions. In my view, the first talk on patient education was the most useful one today. I think clear communication matters more than any new device, and I believe doctors should spend more time listening to their patients. Some colleagues prefer the technical lectures, and I understand that point of view, but I would rather attend the practical workshops. Honestly, I find that I learn more when I can ask questions and discuss real cases. Of course, it depends on the topic. For complex science, a lecture is fine; for daily practice, I prefer a small group. That is just my opinion, but I am sure many of you feel the same.

At a congress, I always try to share my opinion in a polite and open way. First, I listen carefully to what the other person believes. Then, if I agree, I say so clearly, because it builds a good connection. If I disagree, I never say you are wrong. Instead, I say something softer, like I see your point, but I think there is another side. I always give a reason for my opinion, and I use phrases such as in my view or I would say. I also ask the other person what they think, because a good discussion goes both ways. Honestly, sharing opinions with respect is one of the best ways to learn from other doctors.

extra_audio.text: Here is how I share my opinion in a discussion. First, I listen carefully to the other person's point of view. Then I agree or disagree in a polite way. After that, I give a clear reason for what I think. Next, I add my own preference and say what I would rather do. Finally, I ask the other person what they think, so the conversation goes both ways.
